title: Monomeric and Polymeric Tetra-aminophthalocyanatocobalt(II) Modified Electrodes: Electrocatalytic Reduction of Oxygen
abstract:
Research Article
Monomeric and Polymeric
Tetra-aminophthalocyanatocobalt(II) Modiﬁed
Electrodes: Electrocatalytic Reduction of Oxygen
YU-HONG TSE, PAVEL JANDA†, HERMAN LAM, JIUJUN ZHANG, WILLIAM J. PIETRO and A.B.P. LEVER
Department of Chemistry, York University, North York, Ontario, Canada, M3J 1P3
Received 8 August 1996
Accepted 14 August 1996
ABSTRACT: The monomeric and polymeric tetra-aminophthalocyane to, cobalt(II) species adsorbed onto
graphite electrodes are active in electrocatalytic oxygen reduction. While the monomeric species is unstable,
the polymerized species is an effective and stable reduction catalyst over a wide pH range. Both the
two-electron reduction of oxygen to hydrogen peroxide and the four-electron reduction of oxygen to water
are characterized by cyclic voltammetry, rotating disc and rotating ring-disc studies with appropriate
theoretical analysis. Some mechanistic information is obtained. This is the ﬁrst cobalt phthalocyanine species
to provide a four-electron reduction pathway which exists over a wide pH range and is stable. The stability
is associated with the polymerization since the monomeric species is not stable. © 1997 by John Wiley &
Sons, Ltd.
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines, Vol.1, 3–16 (1997) (No. of ﬁgures: 12 No. of tables: 5 No. of refs: 57)
KEYWORDS: 
tetra-aminophthalocyanato 
cobalt(II); 
monomer, 
polymer; 
modiﬁed 
electrodes;
electrocatalysis; oxygen reduction
1 INTRODUCTION
In recent years there have been many publications
on the use of variously substituted cobalt phthalo-
cyanines in the electrocatalytic reduction of oxygen,
mostly relating to the formation of hydrogen per-
oxide the two-electron process [1–24]. In some cases,
a second reduction process at more negative poten-
tials is observed, corresponding with the further
reduction of hydrogen peroxide to water. Only very
rarely has the direct four-electron reduction of
oxygen to water been claimed to be catalysed by a
cobalt phthalocyanine [5, 7, 8,15]. It is this process
which is of most importance with respect to the
replacement of platinum as a catalyst in a fuel cell
[13].
This report illustrates some new, or less common
features of cobalt phthalocyanine catalysed reduc-
tion of oxygen, especially the four-electron reduction
of oxygen to water, the use of a polymerized
phthalocyanine, the variability of behaviour as a
function of the degree of oxidation of the polymer,
and a brief comparison with the behaviour of the
unpolymerized surface.
This is the ﬁrst example of a cobalt phthalocyanine
catalysed four-electron reduction of oxygen which is
valid from low to high pH (2 through 13) and which
is stable to repeated scanning. This is a property of
the polymerized cobalt phthalocyanine species since
the monomeric adsorbed tetra-amino phthalocyani-
nato cobalt(II) (CoTAPc) does not display a stable
four-electron reduction process. Other methods of
cobalt phthalocyanine catalysed four-electron reduc-
tion of oxygen suffer from decomposition over a few
cyclic scans [7], or have only been reported in a high
pH range [5]. In another four-electron reduction
study [15], where this process occurs at a potential
†Current address: J. Heyrovsky Institute of Physical Chemistry,
Academy of Sciences of the Czech Republic, Dolejskova 3, 182
23 Prague 8, Czech Republic
Address for correspondence: A. B. P. Lever, Department of
Chemistry, York University, North York, Ontario, Canada, M3J
1P3
Journal of Porphyrins and Phthalocyanines, Vol. 1, 3–16 (1997)
CCC 1088-4246/97/010003–14 $17.50
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
 negative of the two-electron reduction process, as in
this work, hydrogen peroxide was implicated as an
intermediate, and deﬁnitive data could not be
obtained at high pH. However polymer incorporated
cobalt phthalocyanine apparently provides a four-
electron reduction process over a comparatively
wide pH range [8], but its long term stability was not
cited.
Guarr and coworkers [25–30] have previously
characterized the electropolymerization of CoTAPc
to generate a polymeric phthalocyanine modiﬁed
electrode, termed p-CoTAPc here. They also
recorded its electrochemical or electrocatalytic activ-
ity towards the oxidation of hydroquinone and the
reduction of Mn2(CO)10 [26], the catalytic oxidation
of oxalic acid [27], the amperometric detection of
several thiols [28] and the oxidation of reduced
nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide (NADH) [30].
They have also demonstrated reversible electro-
chromic behaviour [29]. There are some differences,
as we will describe, however, in the electrochemical
behaviour reported by Guarr, and noted in our
laboratory. These differences seem to be related to
the extent of oxidation of the polymer.
Repetitive cycling around the Co(II)TAPc ring
oxidation 
potential 
in 
N,N-dimethylformamide
(DMF) solution results in the oxidative polymeriza-
tion of Co(II)TAPc and the deposition of an
electroactive polymer onto the electrode surface [25,
26, 31]; the polymeric Co(II)TAPc modiﬁed elec-
trode is termed p-Co(II)TAPc/C in this report. We
have previously considered the possible use of the
p-Co(II)TAPc/C electrode as a sensor for the deter-
mination of the sulphide ion [32], and therefore have
studied the surface electrochemical behaviour of
both monomeric and polymerized Co(II)TAPc
absorbed on various graphite electrodes, and at
various pH values.
2 EXPERIMENTAL
2.1 Materials
Tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP; Kodak)
was recrystallized from absolute ethanol and dried in
a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 2 days. DMF (Aldrich)
and all other chemicals were reagent grade or better
and were used as supplied. All experiments were
carried out at ambient laboratory temperature.
Water was puriﬁed by double distillation with
KMNO4, followed by passage through the Barnstead
organic removal cartridge and two Barnstead mixed-
resin Ultrapure cartridges. Buffer solutions were
prepared 
from 
0.1 
M
solutions 
of 
H3PO4,
KH2PO4K2HPO4, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaOH and
KOH, and were adjusted to the desired pH.
Argon (Linde) and oxygen (Linde) were used as
supplied. The buffer solutions were saturated with
dioxygen or argon and maintained under an atmos-
phere 
of 
the 
appropriate 
gas 
during 
the
experiments.
2.2 Methods
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using
either the Pine Instruments RDE-3 potentiostat and
the Pine Instruments PIR rotator or the Wenking
Model ST72 potentiostat coupled with a Mars II
programmed voltage generator (J. Heyrovsky Insti-
tute) and an x-y recorder (EMG 79812, Hungary).
Various graphite electrodes, highly oriented pyr-
olytic graphite (HOPG), or glassy carbon (GC) were
used and treated in slightly different fashions,
depending on the experiment. Note that a HOPG
electrode was used for monomeric Co(II)TAPc and
for p-Co(II)TAPc obtained by direct oxidative
polymerization of the monomeric monolayer sur-
face, while the GC electrode was used for thicker
layers of p-Co(II)TAPc obtained by oxidative
polymerization with the electrode dipped into Co(II)
TAPc/DMF solution.
A conventional three-electrode cell was used in
electrochemical experiments. HOPG and GC elec-
trodes were mounted in Teﬂon with an exposed
circular area of 0.495 and 0.196 cm2, respectively, as
the working electrodes. Before each experiment, the
surface of the HOPG electrode was cleaned by
removing several layers of the surface using adhesive
tape. The GC electrode was polished with diamond
paste and then polished with a clean polishing cloth
and washed with water and ethanol.
Platinum and AgCl/Ag wires were used as the
counter and quasi-reference electrodes, respectively.
The ring-disc electrode consisted of a glassy carbon
disc and a platinum ring. The distances from the
centre of the disc to the disc edge, inner edge and
outer ring edge were 0.382, 0.399, 0.422 cm, respec-
tively. The calculated collection efﬁciency of 0.18 was
conﬁrmed 
by 
experiments 
with 
solutions 
of
[Fe(CN)6]3–.
In the aqueous buffer solutions, all the measure-
ments were recorded against a saturated calomel
electrode (SCE) or a mercurous sulphate reference
electrode and all the data reported here are against

YU-HONG TSE, PAVEL JANDA†, HERMAN LAM, JIUJUN ZHANG, WILLIAM J. PIETRO and A.B.P. LEVER

graphite electrodes are active in electrocatalytic oxygen reduction. While the monomeric species is unstable,

are characterized by cyclic voltammetry, rotating disc and rotating ring-disc studies with appropriate
theoretical analysis. Some mechanistic information is obtained. This is the ﬁrst cobalt phthalocyanine species
to provide a four-electron reduction pathway which exists over a wide pH range and is stable. The stability
is associated with the polymerization since the monomeric species is not stable. © 1997 by John Wiley &

a SCE if no other reference is stated. Potentials were

Electronic spectra were recorded with a Cary

The synthesis and spectroscopic characterization of
this species have been previously reported [32].
2.4 Adsorption study and preparation of the
For the adsorption of Co(II)TAPc onto the HOPG
or basal-plane pyrolytic graphite (BPG) electrode
surface, the electrode was immersed in a 1 3 10–4 M
solution of Co(II)TAPc in DMF for 5 min, removed
from the solution and washed with double distilled
water before being transferred to the electrochem-
The p-Co(II)TAPc electrode was prepared by
following a literature method [25, 26, 31]. A GC
electrode (0.196 cm2) was scanned in Co(II)TAPc
solution (1 3 10–4 M) using DMF as solvent from the
range of 0 to 1.0 V (vs. AgCl/AG) under argon-
degassed conditions. The thickness of the ﬁlm can be
controlled by varying the length of time for electro-
polymerization. The electrode was then rinsed with
ethanol and water before being transferred to the
3.1 Surface Electrochemistry of Monomeric
Co(II)TAPc on the HOPG Electrode under
Studies of the surface electrochemistry of metal-
lophthalocyanine species provide a rapid and easy
means to explore their electrochemical properties
[21–23, 33–38], in the absence of potentially compli-
cating solution equilibria. The surface electrochem-
ical behaviour of adsorbed monomeric Co(II)TAPc
(not polymerized) was studied on several different
graphite surfaces: HOPG and BPG. While the
behaviour of the polymerized species was independ-
ent of the graphite employed the most clearly
resolved voltammograms of the monomeric species
were obtained on BPG. Some data on HOPG are
reported in this paper for comparison with the
voltammetry of the polymer attached to HOPG,
while a more detailed assessment of CoTAPc/BPG is
Figure 1 shows the cyclic voltammogram of
adsorbed Co(II)TAPc on an HOPG electrode,
(described as the Co(II)TAPc/HOPG electrode), at
pH 1.65, 7 and 13. Scanning the Co(II)TAPc/HOPG
electrode negative of zero at pH 7 shows a moder-
ately well-deﬁned reversible redox couple at about
– 0.59 V, labelled as III (Fig. 1 ii; Table 1), at a
potential generally associated with the Co(II)Pc(2-
Fig. 1. Surface cyclic voltammogram of adsorbed
(monomeric) Co(II)TAPc/HOPG modiﬁed electrode
in argon-degassed conditions; scan rate: 100 mV s–1.
(i) pH 1.65; equilibrium scan from 0.8 to –0.6 V; surface
coverage (calculated from anodic wave of couple
III before the appearance of couples I and II, see
text): 1.25 3 10–10 mol cm–2. (ii) pH 7; surface cov-
erage: 1.24 3 10–10 mol cm–2; (a) equilibrium scan
from 0 to –1.0 V; (b) ﬁrst scan (dotted line) ongoing to
0.6 V after the equilibrium scan (a) is obtained; (c)
equilibrium scan (dashed line) from 0.6 to –1.0 V. (iii)
pH 13; surface coverage: 1.0 3 10–10 mol cm–2;
equilibrium scan from 0.3 to –1.1 V. Insert: surface
cyclic voltammogram of the p-Co(II)TAPc surface on
a GC electrode in pH 7 argon-degassed conditions;
scan rate: 100 mV s–1; equilibrium scan from 0.2 to
TETRA-AMINO PHTHALOCYANATO COBALT(II) MODIFIED ELECTRODES
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Table 1. Variation of redox potentials for adsorbed cobalt phthalocyanine derivatives
* Cyclic voltammogram under argon; average of anodic and cathodic waves rate: 100 mV
† The redox potential of couple III is determined before the appearance of couples I and II.
)/[Co(I)Pc(2-)]– couple [40]. The anodic and cathodic
peak currents of this couple are proportional to the
scan rate, consistent with the electrochemical behav-
iour for a surface adsorbed species. The redox
potential of this couple is almost pH independent in
the alkaline region and shifts about –60 mV per pH
unit in the acidic range. This pH dependence is as
anticipated [40] for the Co(II)Pc(2-)/[Co(I)Pc(2-)]–
couple. Further, a Hammett plot of the Co(II)Pc(2-
)/[Pc(2-)Co(I)]– couple for variously substituted
cobalt phthalocyanines [41–43] (Fig. 2) shows that
data for Co(II)TAPc obeys the correlation well,
The slope obtained from this Hammett plot is
0.048 V/δp (R2 = 0.99), a value only about half that
of the slope for the cobalt phthalocyanine species in
solution (0.1 V/δp) [41]. Probably the strong inter-
action between the phthalocyanine ring and the
graphite structure on the electrode surface dilutes
the effect of the substituent.
Considering couple III as a one-electron redox
couple, the surface concentration of that particular
surface is 1.24 3 10–10 mol cm–2. Assuming that the
phthalocyanine molecule lies ﬂat on the electrode
surface yielding an area of about 200 ˚A2, the
coverage, Γ, is about one monolayer [12, 21].
If this Co(II)TAPc/HOPG electrode is then
scanned at potentials positive of couple III, a
dramatic increase of the anodic current is observed
on the ﬁrst scan (Fig. 1), due to polymerization of the
surface Co(II)TAPc via the amino functions (vide
infra). During successive cycling until a steady scan
was reached, two new additional redox couples grew
in at 0.42 and 0.16 V, labelled as I and II, respectively
(pH 1.65) (Fig. 1i, Table 1). In acidic condition, the
equilibrium is attained in a few scans, more rapidly
During the development of couples I and II, there
is no major change in the appearance of couple III at
pH 1.65 and 7 indicating that this couple is largely
insensitive to the polymerization of Co(II)TAPc on
the electrode surface (Fig. 1i and ii). However, at pH
13 the appearance of couple III was affected by the
overlap of couple II which appeared in a similar
redox potential range (Fig. 1iii).
Since couples I and II appear only after scanning
to positive potential, they must correspond to the
oxidized (polymerized) product of Co(II)TAPc on
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
the surface. The area under couple I is approximately
twice that under couple III, while couple II has
about 20% the area of couple III.
3.2 Surface Electrochemistry of p-Co(II)TAPc
on the GC Electrode under Argon
Figure 3 shows the repetitive cyclic voltammogram
(CV) of a solution of Co(II)TAPc in DMF at around
0 to 1.0 V (vs. AgCl/Ag). The gradual increase of the
peak current near 0.4 V, indicates that electro-
polymerization of the Co(II)TAPc has occurred on
the GC electrode to form the p-Co(II)TAPc/GC
modiﬁed electrode. This process of polymerization
does not depend on the type of carbon electrode
used; both HOPG and GC electrodes showed similar
In contrast to Guarr’s observation [27, 28], if the
p-Co(II)TAPc/GC electrode is transferred to an
aqueous buffer solution, the CV of this modiﬁed
electrode shows three redox couples (Fig. 4) on the
ﬁrst scan. After comparing the result observed for
the adsorbed Co(II)TAPc/HOPG modiﬁed elec-
trode, these three redox couples were assigned as
I–III as described for the adsorbed Co(II)TAPc
An interesting facet of these observations is that
Fig. 2. Plot of Co(II)Pc(2-)/Co(I)Pc(2-) redox poten-
tials for the adsorbed cobalt phthalocyanine deriva-
tives at pH 13 (except for CoPc species for which the
Co(II)Pc(2-)/[Co(I)Pc(2-)]– redox potential at pH 14
condition is used) vs. the Hammett Σδ values.
CoTNPc, tetraneopentoxyphthalocyanatocobalt(II);
CoTSPc, tetrasulfonatophthalocyanatocobalt(II).
Fig. 3. Repetitive cyclic voltammograms of (0 to 1.0 V
vs. AgCl/Ag) 1 3 10–4 M Co(II)TAPc solution in DMF/
0.1 M TBAP at a GC electrode (0.196 cm2); scan rate
Fig. 4. Surface cyclic voltammogram of p-Co(II)TAPc/
GC modiﬁed electrode, prepared as in Fig. 3, in
argon-saturated pH 10 phosphate buffer solution,
scan rate: 12.5, 25, 50, 75, 100 mV. s–1.

Co(II)TAPc modified electrode under argon
condition as a function of pH*
* Cyclic voltammogram under argon; average of
anodic and cathodic waves at scan rate: 100
the electrochemistry of the polymerized monolayer
adsorbed Co(II)TAPc discussed in Section 3.1 is
essentially identical to the electrochemistry of the
growing polymer generated in Co(II)TAPc/DMF.
Thus an adsorbed monolayer is able to ‘cross-link’
on the surface in a manner reminiscent of the in situ
polymerization of Langmuir monolayers [44].
The thickness of the polymer ﬁlm may be con-
trolled by altering the repetitive cycling time [28].
Increasing the time from 2 to 10 min in 1 3 10–4 M
Co(II)TAPc/DMF solution does not show any sig-
niﬁcant resolution of these redox couples but the
residual current increases with increasing thickness
of the ﬁlm. This behaviour has been observed for
related polymerization processes with, for example,
Couples I and II shift by –56 and –70 mV per pH
unit, respectively, in the whole pH range (Table 2,
Figure 5). However, at pH 13 the appearance of
couple III was affected by overlap with couple II
which appeared in a similar redox potential range
Between about pH 6 and 10, the peak position of
couple III does not change much with pH but in the
acidic range, couple III shifts by –56 mV per pH unit,
i.e. behaviour analogous to the monomeric surface.
The appearance of couples I–III is dependent on the
switching limit of the CV. If the scan limit of the
p-Co(II)TAPc/GC electrode (Fig. 6ii) is switched to
the potential about 400 mV positive of couple I,
there is a gradual decrease in the current of couples
I and III but there is no signiﬁcant change in the
current of couple II. At the equilibrium scan, couples
Fig. 5. pH dependences of the redox potentials of
couples I, II and III, and the oxygen reduction peaks
V and VI for a p-Co(II)TAPc/GC ﬁlm (see Tables 2,
Fig. 6. Surface cyclic voltammogram of p-Co(II)TAPc/
GC modiﬁed electrode in argon-degassed pH 1.65
buffer solution as a function of positive switching
potential; scan rate: 100 mV s–1 (preparation condi-
tions; scan time: 5 min in 1 3 10–4 M Co(II)TAPc/DMF
solution/0.1 M TBAP, see the experimental section).
© 1997 by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Table 3. Voltammetric data for cobalt phthalocyanine derivative modified electrodes under argon
and dioxygen conditions as a function of pH*
*The peak position is determined at scan rate 100 mV/s.
I and III are only weakly observed (Fig. 6ii).
Moreover, if the switching limit is extended to a
potential 600 mV positive of couple I, couple II will
also disappear and a new redox couple, labelled IV,
appears at the potential between couple II and III
The reversible couple which appears at 0.48 V at
pH 2.13 after ﬁrst scanning a freshly prepared
p-Co(II)TAPc/C ﬁlm to 1.0 V, reported by Guarr [25,
26], is probably the same as couple IV observed in
this work. Most importantly we demonstrate below
that a p-Co(II)TAP ﬁlm which shows couple IV, has
signiﬁcantly different electroactivity towards the
reduction of the dioxygen compared with a p-Co(II)
TAPc ﬁlm which exhibits couples I–III.
3.2.1 Couples I and II Although the precise struc-
ture of p-CoTAPc is unknown, the electropolymer-
ization most likely involves oxidation of the amino
groups to form radicals which then attack the
benzene rings of neighbouring molecules. It is likely
that the polymer will have the characteristics of
polyaniline, which exhibits two couples in the same
region as p-CoTAPc [47]. Thus couples I and II may
be tentatively assigned to the oxidation of polyani-
line-like components of the polymer ﬁlm. Probably,
however, they also contain a contribution from the
ﬁrst-ring oxidation process, Pc(1-)/Pc(2-), as sug-
gested by Jin in his study of p-H2TAPc ﬁlms [48]. The
similarity of the data between p-H2TAPc and p-Co-
TAPc suggests that the metal is not involved.
In general [1–24], CoPc species catalyse the two-
electron reduction of oxygen to hydrogen peroxide
and a subsequent two-electron reduction of hydro-
gen peroxide to water. Table 3 shows some literature
data of the comparative potentials for these proc-
esses. The reduction potentials are pH dependent,
being most positive under acidic conditions. Near pH
2, the ﬁrst reduction occurs in the range 0.1–0.3 V,
while the separation from the second reduction
varies quite dramatically (see Table 3).
In a very few cases, the four-electron reduction of
oxygen to water [5, 7, 8, 15] has been reported, but in
general these studies involve unstable catalyst sur-
faces and/or are restricted to a narrow pH range.
This four-electron reduction is, however, the desir-
able process for fuel cell applications [13] and has
been observed in a number of especially designed
cobalt porphyrins species [49]. It is also important,
for fuel cell purposes, that this process occur at as
positive a potential as possible.
3.3.1 p-Co(II)TAPc Surface We observe that the
p-Co(II)TAPc surface which shows couples I–III has
the ability to catalyse O2 reduction both by a two-

electron pathway to hydrogen peroxide and a four-
electron pathway to water. However, the more
oxidized p-Co(II)TAPc/C surface which shows cou-
ple IV only, catalyses O2 reduction by a two-electron

a p-Co(II)TAPc/GC electrode in a dioxygen-sat-

waves, labelled as V and VI, are observed. The
currents for these two waves are proportional to the
square root of the scan rate indicative of the
diffusion waves. Waves V and VI are assigned as the
reduction of oxygen to hydrogen peroxide and
oxygen to water, respectively, as will be demon-
The general appearance of this CV does not
depend signiﬁcantly on the thickness of the ﬁlm.
Thus the p-Co(II)TAPc/GC electrodes which are
prepared by scanning the base GC electrode for 2 or
10 min in Co(II)TAPc/DMF solution (0.1 M TBAP;
scan range: 0 to 1 V vs. AgCl/Ag; scan rate: 100 mV
s–1 show almost identical CV (within experimental
error) in 0.1 M H3PO4 dioxygen-saturated solution.
The assignment of wave V is unambiguous since
cobalt phthalocyanine is well known to catalyse O2
to H2O2 [1–24]. However, the assignment of wave VI
is not so obvious. It could represent the process
oxygen to hydrogen peroxide followed by reduction
of hydrogen peroxide to water (2 + 2 process) since
cobalt phthalocyanines are known to catalyse the

where nα is the number of electrons involved in the

1.65, 10 and 13 shows three straight lines (Fig. 8) with

these slopes, values of αnα were determined to be

These numbers indicate that the mechanism for
dioxygen reduction to hydrogen peroxide catalysed
by a p-Co(II)TAPc/GC electrode proceeds through
a one-electron rate-determining step with an α value
of approximately 0.5 in acidic medium [12, 51] and
either involves a two-electron rate-determining step
with a α value of about 0.5 or a one-electron transfer
step with an α value of approximately 1 in strong
base condition [52]. For other phthalocyanine deriv-
atives [12, 16, 17–20, 22], αnα equal to 0.5 has been

This behaviour is different, as far as we are aware,
from all other CoPc species where the cyclic voltam-
mogram shows two successive oxygen reduction
processes but the dynamic RDE study shows only
the ﬁrst reduction wave (e.g. Ref. 22, Fig. 5). The
usual loss of the second RDE wave is due to the fact
that the H2O2 formed at the surface of the CoTNPc/
C electrode [22] upon reduction of oxygen is spun off
the surface before there is time for it to be reduced.
The observation of the second RDE wave in this
p-CoTAPc/C example must arise because either
there is a direct four-electron reduction to water, or
there is a 2 + 2 process but the hydrogen peroxide
which is formed is reduced extremely rapidly. We

A rotating ring-disc electrode (RRDE) experi-
ment was performed at pH 1.65 (Fig. 12). The disc
potential was scanned from a potential positive of
couple V to negative of couple VI while the ring was
held at a potential appropriate for the oxidation of
hydrogen peroxide. Ring current indicating the
generation of hydrogen peroxide was observed when
the disc was scanned through couple V, but very little
ring current was observed as the disc was scanned
through couple VI. This experiment provides the
ﬁnal proof that couple V involves oxygen reduction
to hydrogen peroxide, while couple VI is the four-
electron reduction of oxygen to water. Note that this
result is in accord with an earlier study [15] of a two-
electron process followed at more negative poten-
tials by a four-electron process for a CoPc/poly-

Mechanism of Oxygen Reduction by p-Co(II)
TAPc Film It is tempting to assume that couple II,
involving a redox process at the polymer ﬁlm
framework, is responsible for the oxygen reduction
catalytic activity since wave V appears jut 100–300
mV positive or negative of the oxygen reduction
process depending on the pH (see Tables 2 and 3,
and Fig. 5). However, based upon previous experi-
ence, it is far more probable that the cobalt centre is
Studies of oxygen reduction catalysed by CoTNPc
[12] or by CoCRPc [21–23] show that the oxygen
reduction pH dependence parallels that of the
Co(II)Pc(2-)/Co(I)Pc(2-) couple, but usually at more
positive potentials. However, we [23, 35] have
argued, in line with a much earlier seminal study of
the mechanism of CoPc catalysed oxygen reduction
[17], that the active species is probably the divalent
Co(II)Pc(2-) instead of the Co(I)Pc(2-) species and
an intermediate oxygen adduct is involved:
This argument is based on the observation that
oxygen reduction, catalysed by the CoCl16Pc species
[35], appears at a potential almost 400–600 mV more
positive than the Co(II)Cl16Pc(2-)/Co(I)Cl16Pc(2-)
couple in strong alkaline condition. In this case the
Co(I)Cl16Pc(2-) species could not be the active
catalyst since such a positive oxygen reduction
potential would require an unrealistically high

lower temperature [57]. The fact that the pH

In a similar manner to the CoTNPc, CoCl16Pc and

Although the potential of VI appears rather

close vicinity of couple III. This result suggests that
involved in the four-electron reduction process to
water via a reaction product with [Co(I)Pc(-2)]–
similar to that described in Eq. (5).
3.3.2 Co(II)TAPc Monomer The p-Co(II)TAPc sur-
face shows activity for catalysing the four-electron
reduction process of O2 to water. However, is this
the true electroactivity of the p-Co(II)TAPc ﬁlm
itself or could it possibly be due to the electroactivity
of Co(II)TAPc monomer trapped in the polymer
form? To ﬁnd out, the electroreduction of O2
catalysed by monomeric Co(II)TAPc absorbed on
HOPG electrode has also been brieﬂy studied.
The unpolymerized surface is not a very good
oxygen reduction electrocatalyst. Under CV condi-
tions, two reduction processes are seen [at –0.6 and
–0.68 V at pH 1.65 (not shown)] on the ﬁrst CV cycle,
but on the second CV cycle, the second reduction
wave disappears, probably indicating some destruc-
Further, an RDE study shows only one two-
electron wave in the dioxygen-saturated condition,
O2 →H2O2, with the monomeric Co(II)TAPc/
HOPG electrode (Figure 12b). Thus the polymeric
species is required for the four-electron oxygen
The surface electrochemistry of adsorbed and poly-
meric Co(II)TAPc on graphite electrode has been
investigated in different pH aqueous buffer solu-
tions. The detailed CV behaviour of both the
adsorbed monomeric, and polymeric Co(II)TAPc
species is strongly dependent on the switching
The p-Co(II)TAPc/C electrode is found to be an
effective catalytic surface for O2 reduction both to
hydrogen peroxide and directly in the four-electron
reduction of oxygen to water. While this process
occurs at a potential too negative to be of use in a
fuel cell application, it is signiﬁcant that the use of a
polymerized CoPc species provides a stable four-
electron reduction catalyst when the corresponding
monomeric CoPc surface is very unstable towards
oxygen reduction, and only participates in the two-
